items.q: Why must the 14 major allergens be highlighted on UK food labels?
items.correct: Reactions can be severe or life-threatening, so consumers must identify them at a glance.
items.misconception: To advertise that the food contains them.
items.why: Students miss the consumer-safety logic. Labelling laws require allergens to be visibly listed (bold/highlighted) so allergic people can quickly check. Penalties for non-compliance can be severe.
Students confuse fat- and water-soluble vitamins. Mnemonic: ADEK fat-soluble (stored in body fat - can build up to toxic levels). B and C water-soluble (excess excreted in urine).

items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: Does a triangle test tell you which product people prefer?
items.correct: No - it only tests whether tasters can DETECT a difference between two products; preference needs a separate test (e.g. paired preference or hedonic rating).
items.misconception: Yes - whichever sample is picked as the odd one out is the preferred product.
items.random_wrong: Triangle tests measure the volume of the samples.
items.why: Students blur discrimination tests with preference tests. The triangle test asks 'is there a perceptible difference?' - useful when reformulating (e.g. cutting salt). Liking is measured separately with hedonic scales or preference ranking.

items.q: Are different tastes detected only in fixed zones of the tongue (the 'tongue map')?
items.correct: No - the tongue map is a debunked myth; all basic tastes (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) can be detected across the tongue.
items.misconception: Yes - sweet is sensed only at the tip, bitter only at the back, and so on.
items.random_wrong: Taste is detected by the teeth, not the tongue.
items.why: The tongue-map diagram still appears in older books but came from a mistranslated 1901 study. Taste buds across the tongue respond to all five basic tastes (sensitivity varies only slightly by region) - and flavour also depends heavily on aroma.

items.q: Flavour is mainly the combination of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Taste and aroma. “Sight and sound” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: To avoid bias from labels. “To save money” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Number of senses used in full sensory analysis?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
